title: STANDARD FOR CHAYOTES CXS 216-1999 Adopted in 1999. Amended in 2005, 2011.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR CHAYOTES CXS 216-1999 Adopted in 1999. Amended in 2005, 2011.
sections.summary: Document title: Codex Standard for Chayotes (CXS 216‑1999), adopted 1999 and amended 2005, 2011.
Definition/scope: Standard applies to commercial varieties of chayotes (Sechium edule) supplied fresh after preparation and packaging and excludes chayotes for industrial processing.

Provisions concerning quality: lists minimum requirements (whole, sound, clean, pest‑free, free of abnormal moisture, foreign odours/tastes, firm, fresh appearance, free from various damages, spines and germination), maturity/transport suitability and classifies chayotes into Extra, I and II with specified allowable superficial defects.

Minimum requirements: chayotes must be whole, sound, clean, practically free of pests and pest damage, free of abnormal external moisture, foreign smell/taste, firm, fresh in appearance, free of temperature and sun damage, fibrous flesh, hard spines and visible germination, and adequately developed to withstand transport.
Classification overview: chayotes are classified into three classes (Extra, I, II) with specific allowances for slight or greater superficial defects as defined in subsequent subsections.

'Extra' Class for chayotes: superior quality, characteristic of variety/commercial type, free of defects except very slight superficial defects that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I for chayotes: good quality and characteristic of the variety/commercial type, allowing slight shape defects (light grooves/depressions), slight colouring defects up to 25% surface, and slight scarring up to 3 cm², provided pulp is unaffected.

Class II for chayotes: includes produce meeting minimum requirements but not higher classes, allowing shape defects, colouring defects up to 35% surface and scarring up to 5 cm², provided pulp is unaffected and essential characteristics remain.

Sizing for chayotes: size determined by weight or length with minimums (200 g or 12 cm) and size codes A–D defined by weight/length ranges; for size code D, individual fruit weight differences may not exceed 150 g.

Tolerances: allows per‑package quality and size tolerances—Extra class 5% non‑conforming to class (but meeting Class I), Class I 10% (but meeting Class II), Class II 10% (except rotting), and size tolerances of 10% for adjacent sizes.

Quality tolerances details: repeats permitted percentages—Extra 5% by number/weight, Class I 10%, Class II 10% non‑conforming (excluding rotting) with conformity details as in main tolerances section.
Extra class tolerance: up to 5% by number or weight of chayotes not meeting Extra class but meeting Class I or its tolerances.
Class I tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of chayotes not meeting Class I but meeting Class II or its tolerances.
Class II tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of chayotes may fail to meet Class II or minimum requirements, except produce affected by rotting or otherwise unfit for consumption.
Size tolerances (chayotes): for all classes up to 10% by number or weight may correspond to the size immediately above or below that indicated on the package.

Sets presentation requirements for chayotes, requiring uniform contents by variety/commercial type, origin, quality, colour and size; mandates protective, hygienic packaging with new or food-grade recycled materials, non-toxic printing, compliance with CXC 44‑1995, and containers with appropriate quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance free of foreign matter and odour.

Requires each package of chayotes to be uniform and contain only chayotes of the same variety/commercial type, origin, quality, colour and size, and that the visible portion be representative of the whole.

Requires chayotes to be packed to protect the produce using new (or food‑grade recycled), clean materials that do not cause damage, allows printed/stamped materials only with non‑toxic ink or glue, mandates compliance with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport (CXC 44‑1995), and requires containers to meet hygiene, ventilation and strength criteria and be free of foreign matter and smell.
